transcript.transcript: Hi, I'm Tom Brown and I'm volunteering with the Emmet Orchards Oral History Project. Uh, today, Sunday, March 1st, 2026. And I'm in Beaverton, Oregon, in the home of my second cousin, Tess Rossow and her husband, Scott. And, um, today we're going to be talking about Tessa's early life in Emmet and her experience, uh, in family orchards there and the heritage that's flown out of that, uh, this oral histories conducted with the support of the Idaho Historical Society as part of Idaho's commemoration of America 250. Tess, uh, first, just let me thank you for inviting me into your home. Oh, you're entirely welcome. It's my pleasure. We've had a great visit already. Yes. And, um, uh, This and thank you for your time for this interview, which allows you to reflect on your Emmet heritage and share your experience and memories. So before we jump into the questions, do you give permission to record this interview? I do. And, uh, could you state and spell your first and last name, please? My first name is Theresa. T h e r e s a and I go by Tess, which is t e s s. And my last name is Rossow r o s s o w. Well, Tess, um, our great grandfather, uh, moved to Emmet in 1907, planted orchards on the south slope of the valley. Um, each of our grandparents, my grandmother, your grandfather, Hazel Wills Brown, uh, Frank Weston Wills, uh, inherited and purchased orchards there and, um, you, you grew up there as well. Now a lot of miles and time has happened. And, um, our grandparents, who grew up side by side, um, were close. But our branch of the family's maintained some connection over the years. And I happen to know that my parents, Maurice and Ruth Brown, had a wonderful time when you lived in New Jersey? Yes. Tell us about that. So, um, it was not long after my dad's funeral, and, of course, I had connected with your parents at that time, and they mentioned that they were coming to visit you in Egypt, and they would be going through New York. And I invited them to come spend some time with us in New York. Um, New Jersey. Both my husband and I worked in the city, but lived in New Jersey, and we just had a marvelous time with your parents. They just so loving and fun and, um, you know, set them up on this tour around, um, New York so they could see around, had meals with them, just hung out and chatted and talked and just had a really wonderful time with them. You mentioned it was an emotional time. Yes. How many? But maybe you can back up a bit to talk about the death, the untimely death of your father, and, uh, and then the comfort of my visiting parents. So, um, my dad's birthday was May 15th. And on May 18th, um, at the age of 58, he suffered a massive, um, I think it was a stroke. It was something heart related. And, um, he was at my grandmother's house at the time, and he was, um, getting, uh, a rifle together to go shoot squirrels because they were bothering her. And, um, he had this massive stroke and fell on the floor. And because it was my sister who's a nurse, um, had an autopsy performed, And, you know, just to make sure what exactly it was. And, um, yeah. So it was very sudden. The, the really wonderful thing about it was we had all talked to him on his birthday. So we had all had that last conversation, although we didn't know it. Um, with him, um, I was furthest away being in New Jersey. He died in Idaho. Emmett, um, mom was at a conference in Portland at the time. Um, they got hold of my brother Mark to let him know. Um, and he had to drive to Lewis and Clark College and find her and pull her out. Mhm. Um. It was unexpected and, you know, a hard time. And it made me realize that, um, I'd never had kids yet. He'd been waiting. You know, we were living on the East Coast and far away from family. Um, but it made me realize I didn't want to wait any longer. So we actually got pregnant right away. Right away. So when your parents came to visit. Um, so I got pregnant in June, and, um, they came, I believe. Actually, I think they came over my birthday. My birthday is August 4th, and I think they were there over my birthday. Makes sense. Yes. I welcome them to Cairo in August. So, yeah, I think they were there over my birthday. Yeah. Um, and now that I think about that. And so they had been lovely, um, when my dad had passed away. So, uh, we jumped right into a major transition in, uh, your family's life. Yeah. Which resulted in the, um. The end of, uh, your family's time in the orchard. You know, there have been some issues about leaving the stove on. Things like that. And she was 90. She really probably shouldn't have been driving. She drove like a bat out of. Hell, double toothpicks. But, um, I really, really, really resented her. Um. When dad died, okay. And felt that she caused his death. Oh, wow. The stress that she put him under caused his death. Later learning more about her life and the things that she went through helped to soften that, um, and help make me understand her better. But it's still there's still that bit of resentment that she was so hard on him and such pressure that she put on him and that division that she caused by, you know, trying to divide him and pull him to the farm. Mhm. Um, and away from, you know, my mom, us. Mhm. You know. It's, it's a, it's a hard thing to um to have that resentment and um, you know, finally found it in myself to forgive her. But it took years. So one of the, uh values that you did learn from her, uh, has to do with hard work. It does? I you know, it's. When it comes to what you have to get done for a job for not necessarily personal stuff that you know you're doing. Like my house is not finished. Drives me crazy, but whatever. Um, your values of you don't leave a job half done. You you finish it. Especially when there are expectations that, um, people are expecting you to to finish something so that they can do their part. You don't just say, I'm tired, I'm going to go home. Uh, or. Oh, gee, I don't really feel well. I don't think I'll come in today. You know, it's more you make yourself do it and get it done and, um. Yeah, yeah, maybe I had a hard night, but I'm not going to take the day off. I'll get grab an extra hour or two of sleep and come in late, but I'm still going to come in. Mhm. You know there's that you, you have things that you need to get done and what you do affects other people. So you darn well better get it done. Mhm. Um I guess uh maybe we'll swing back to the comfort that you felt with my parents. Uh, the, the short weeks after your father's death. What was comforting? Well, okay. Pomommy was never a comforter. Okay. She never never. You know, she would write, I love you, but I don't think she said it. Okay. I don't remember it. She. She was not a warm, fuzzy person by any means. No way. Um, it was a very. You know, she. And you know that. I'm sure it helped her survive a lot of things. Um, but your parents. Warm, loving. And affectionate, I guess would be the best way to put it. Which was very comforting to me after, at that time. You know, it's not like. You know, Pamommy after dad died. It was. What am I going to do? What am I going? What am I going to do? It was never. It was never. Peg, you lost your husband. Tess. You lost your dad. It was always, what am I going to do? Mhm mhm. And so in that respect it was very self centered as to that relationship. And I think maybe that comes down to not willing to share him or. Mhm. Mhm. So well I just want to thank you. And I think we ought to acknowledge what we've been doing this weekend. Can you talk about the project that you've done with your uh your brothers and sister. Um sadly Lisa passed away a number of years ago, but your brothers and your sister Cecilia and some of your nieces and nephews, uh, were were working together. And what do you think your folks or your grandmother would have thought if they could have popped in and seen you together this weekend? Um, so my mom passed away a year ago, and, um, we sold her home, and we, uh, such a project to get, you know, we're in our 60s. We didn't want our furniture. The grandkids didn't want her furniture, for the most part. Um, so it's like, okay, dealing with all of that. And then mom, over the years had collected. Her family history stuff, um, the Wills family history, um, my cousin Agnes Miller's some of her stuff because she never had kids, so we were her beneficiaries. So we ended up with stuff of hers, stuff of families. Um, and so we were going through the last, I don't know, 12, 14, 12, at least 12 boxes of. Pictures and letters and, um, just general memorabilia from all of these people. And my mom took a lot of pictures. She went on a lot of trips and she liked to scrapbook, but she didn't get it all done. And so we threw away a lot of pictures of beautiful places in Europe. But with a lot of people, we didn't know who they were. I mean, she wasn't in the pictures. She took them. So, um, we were, um, sorting things, scanning things, trying to figure out what we're going to do about it. Um, we still have a ways to go. Unfortunately, we spent the weekend. The weekend, the entire weekend. And what might your mom or dad or Pamommy, uh, if they could have peeked in, what would they have seen? What? What might they have thought of you guys this weekend? Well, for the most part, we did pretty well. But there were a couple of arguments there that, you know, there's a lot of tension when you're trying to figure out and when, you know, one person has put a lot of work into this. So, so it puts so much work into everything Mom related, so much work. We are so, so deeply grateful to her for what she did. Invited me to do some help. She did. You know, in that way she's like a mom, you know? She okay? She hooked you in. She hooked me in. Yeah. Yeah, yeah. So, um, I think they would have been proud of us for, um, trying to get this taken care of. Um, not exactly in a timely way. I mean, it's been a year, but, you know, um, the estate''s not closed yet, so, you know, I think we're still doing okay. You're doing okay. Um, well, with that, let's, uh, let's call it a night. Okay. And I just really want to thank you for sharing your history, your stories, and a bit of your heritage that sprung from those orchards. Thank you. And thank you for doing this. We do love having you. Thank you. Um, and. Yeah, I'm glad you're staying. Great. Thank you so much.

lines.line: # Introduction and Interview Permissions

> Tom Brown introduces himself, the oral history project, and the context of the interview with Tess Rossow in Beaverton, Oregon. He explains the project's connection to the Idaho Historical Society and America 250, and thanks Tess for her participation. Before proceeding, he obtains Tess's permission to record the interview and asks her to state and spell her name for the record.

metadata.title: Introduction and Interview Permissions
metadata.synopsis: Tom Brown introduces himself, the oral history project, and the context of the interview with Tess Rossow in Beaverton, Oregon. He explains the project's connection to the Idaho Historical Society and America 250, and thanks Tess for her participation. Before proceeding, he obtains Tess's permission to record the interview and asks her to state and spell her name for the record.

lines.line: # Grandmother's Role in the Community and Family

> The conversation highlights Tess's grandmother's work ethic and leadership, particularly her role in hiring crews for the hog packing house and managing the orchards after being widowed. Tess acknowledges the reliance on migrant labor for harvests and notes her limited knowledge about the specifics of the crews. The discussion underscores the grandmother's resilience and adaptability in the face of hardship.

metadata.title: Grandmother's Role in the Community and Family
metadata.synopsis: The conversation highlights Tess's grandmother's work ethic and leadership, particularly her role in hiring crews for the hog packing house and managing the orchards after being widowed. Tess acknowledges the reliance on migrant labor for harvests and notes her limited knowledge about the specifics of the crews. The discussion underscores the grandmother's resilience and adaptability in the face of hardship.

lines.line: # Family Connections and Shared Memories

> Tom and Tess discuss their shared family heritage, tracing back to their great-grandfather who established orchards in Emmet. They reflect on the connections between their branches of the family, including the close relationship between their grandparents. Tess recalls the visit of Tom's parents to her home in New Jersey, describing the warmth and enjoyment of their time together, especially during a difficult period following her father's death.

metadata.title: Family Connections and Shared Memories
metadata.synopsis: Tom and Tess discuss their shared family heritage, tracing back to their great-grandfather who established orchards in Emmet. They reflect on the connections between their branches of the family, including the close relationship between their grandparents. Tess recalls the visit of Tom's parents to her home in New Jersey, describing the warmth and enjoyment of their time together, especially during a difficult period following her father's death.

lines.line: # The Death of Tess's Father and Its Aftermath

> Tess recounts the sudden and unexpected death of her father at age 58, describing the circumstances and the family's reactions. She shares how the family was geographically dispersed at the time and how her father's passing influenced her own decisions about starting a family. Tess also describes the support and kindness shown by Tom's parents during this challenging period, including their care for her grandmother during the funeral.

metadata.title: The Death of Tess's Father and Its Aftermath
metadata.synopsis: Tess recounts the sudden and unexpected death of her father at age 58, describing the circumstances and the family's reactions. She shares how the family was geographically dispersed at the time and how her father's passing influenced her own decisions about starting a family. Tess also describes the support and kindness shown by Tom's parents during this challenging period, including their care for her grandmother during the funeral.

lines.line: # Emotional Support and Comfort from Extended Family

> Tess contrasts the emotional distance of her grandmother with the warmth and affection she received from Tom's parents after her father's death. She explains that her grandmother was not a comforting presence, focusing instead on her own needs, while Tom's parents provided much-needed support and reassurance during a difficult time.

metadata.title: Emotional Support and Comfort from Extended Family
metadata.synopsis: Tess contrasts the emotional distance of her grandmother with the warmth and affection she received from Tom's parents after her father's death. She explains that her grandmother was not a comforting presence, focusing instead on her own needs, while Tom's parents provided much-needed support and reassurance during a difficult time.

lines.line: # Tess's Father's Upbringing, Career, and Family Moves

> Tess provides a detailed account of her father's early life on the family farm, his struggles with severe allergies, and his educational journey to Whitman College. She describes his career progression from banking in Portland to becoming a national bank examiner, which required frequent travel. The family's moves between Emmet, Boise, Spokane, and Portland are recounted, highlighting the impact on their family life and the reasons behind each relocation.

metadata.title: Tess's Father's Upbringing, Career, and Family Moves
metadata.synopsis: Tess provides a detailed account of her father's early life on the family farm, his struggles with severe allergies, and his educational journey to Whitman College. She describes his career progression from banking in Portland to becoming a national bank examiner, which required frequent travel. The family's moves between Emmet, Boise, Spokane, and Portland are recounted, highlighting the impact on their family life and the reasons behind each relocation.

lines.line: # Siblings and Family Life

> Tess lists her siblings and provides a quick overview of their birth years, noting the close spacing of the children. She shares a family joke about the year they got a television and the advice given to her Catholic parents about living 'like brother and sister.' The conversation touches on the challenges and humor of raising a large family in that era.

metadata.title: Siblings and Family Life
metadata.synopsis: Tess lists her siblings and provides a quick overview of their birth years, noting the close spacing of the children. She shares a family joke about the year they got a television and the advice given to her Catholic parents about living 'like brother and sister.' The conversation touches on the challenges and humor of raising a large family in that era.

lines.line: # Orchard Properties and Family Transitions

> The discussion turns to the family's involvement with various orchard properties in Emmet, including the Old Miller place, the Bucknam property, and the Shaw house. Tess shares memories of living in these homes, family events like birthday parties, and the transition of property ownership. She also mentions the early debates over organic versus conventional farming practices among neighboring families.

metadata.title: Orchard Properties and Family Transitions
metadata.synopsis: The discussion turns to the family's involvement with various orchard properties in Emmet, including the Old Miller place, the Bucknam property, and the Shaw house. Tess shares memories of living in these homes, family events like birthday parties, and the transition of property ownership. She also mentions the early debates over organic versus conventional farming practices among neighboring families.

lines.line: # Childhood Responsibilities and Gender Roles

> Tess describes her responsibilities as the eldest daughter, including household chores and helping with food preparation. She recalls specific memories such as hanging laundry, assisting with butchering, and picking fruit. Tess notes that while chores were generally shared among siblings, there were traditional gender expectations, especially from her grandmother, who assigned certain tasks based on gender and age.

metadata.title: Childhood Responsibilities and Gender Roles
metadata.synopsis: Tess describes her responsibilities as the eldest daughter, including household chores and helping with food preparation. She recalls specific memories such as hanging laundry, assisting with butchering, and picking fruit. Tess notes that while chores were generally shared among siblings, there were traditional gender expectations, especially from her grandmother, who assigned certain tasks based on gender and age.

lines.line: # Grandmother's Life and German Heritage

> Tess recounts the story of her grandmother's family, who emigrated from Germany and settled in Nebraska before moving to Idaho. She describes the strong-willed nature of her great-grandmother, who insisted on owning land for security and stability. Tess reflects on the significant historical changes her grandmother witnessed over her long life, including technological advancements and world events.

metadata.title: Grandmother's Life and German Heritage
metadata.synopsis: Tess recounts the story of her grandmother's family, who emigrated from Germany and settled in Nebraska before moving to Idaho. She describes the strong-willed nature of her great-grandmother, who insisted on owning land for security and stability. Tess reflects on the significant historical changes her grandmother witnessed over her long life, including technological advancements and world events.

lines.line: # Tess's Mother's Background and Adjustment to Rural Life

> Tess describes her mother's upbringing in Takoma Park, Maryland, as the much younger child of older parents. She details her mother's education at private schools and Whitman College, and her initial culture shock upon moving to Emmet. Tess shares anecdotes about her mother's resourcefulness, such as selling World Book encyclopedias to connect with the community, and her influence on Tess's own education.

metadata.title: Tess's Mother's Background and Adjustment to Rural Life
metadata.synopsis: Tess describes her mother's upbringing in Takoma Park, Maryland, as the much younger child of older parents. She details her mother's education at private schools and Whitman College, and her initial culture shock upon moving to Emmet. Tess shares anecdotes about her mother's resourcefulness, such as selling World Book encyclopedias to connect with the community, and her influence on Tess's own education.

lines.line: # Mother's Education, Career, and Community Involvement

> Tess discusses her mother's pursuit of a master's degree in religious education and her subsequent work in various church roles. She highlights her mother's talent for organizing and starting community programs, as well as her involvement in support groups and peace activism. Tess notes her mother's extensive travel and her ability to inspire others to volunteer and participate in community life.

metadata.title: Mother's Education, Career, and Community Involvement
metadata.synopsis: Tess discusses her mother's pursuit of a master's degree in religious education and her subsequent work in various church roles. She highlights her mother's talent for organizing and starting community programs, as well as her involvement in support groups and peace activism. Tess notes her mother's extensive travel and her ability to inspire others to volunteer and participate in community life.

lines.line: # Parenting, Family Values, and Cousin Relationships

> Tess reflects on her decision to start a family soon after her father's death, motivated by a desire for her children to have close relationships with their cousins—something she felt was missing from her own childhood. She describes the importance of family gatherings, vacations, and creating lasting bonds among the next generation. Tess contrasts her children's stable upbringing with her own frequent moves and lack of long-term childhood friendships.

metadata.title: Parenting, Family Values, and Cousin Relationships
metadata.synopsis: Tess reflects on her decision to start a family soon after her father's death, motivated by a desire for her children to have close relationships with their cousins—something she felt was missing from her own childhood. She describes the importance of family gatherings, vacations, and creating lasting bonds among the next generation. Tess contrasts her children's stable upbringing with her own frequent moves and lack of long-term childhood friendships.

lines.line: # Work Experience and Culinary Interests

> Tess outlines her varied work experience, including a brief stint in a cherry packing house, a career in banking, and many years as a Pampered Chef consultant. She discusses her passion for cooking, which developed in part as a reaction to her mother's limited culinary skills. Tess shares stories of her early baking and cooking efforts, her family's 'foodie' culture, and her later transition into property management, inspired by her family's tradition of lifelong learning.

metadata.title: Work Experience and Culinary Interests
metadata.synopsis: Tess outlines her varied work experience, including a brief stint in a cherry packing house, a career in banking, and many years as a Pampered Chef consultant. She discusses her passion for cooking, which developed in part as a reaction to her mother's limited culinary skills. Tess shares stories of her early baking and cooking efforts, her family's 'foodie' culture, and her later transition into property management, inspired by her family's tradition of lifelong learning.

lines.line: # Work Ethic and Responsibility

> Tess describes the strong work ethic instilled in her by her grandmother, emphasizing the importance of completing tasks, reliability, and understanding how one's work affects others. She explains that these values have influenced her approach to both professional and personal responsibilities, shaping her sense of duty and perseverance.

metadata.title: Work Ethic and Responsibility
metadata.synopsis: Tess describes the strong work ethic instilled in her by her grandmother, emphasizing the importance of completing tasks, reliability, and understanding how one's work affects others. She explains that these values have influenced her approach to both professional and personal responsibilities, shaping her sense of duty and perseverance.

lines.line: # Sorting Family History and Reflections on Legacy

> Tess describes the recent family project of sorting through her late mother's belongings, including photographs, letters, and memorabilia from multiple generations. She reflects on the challenges and emotional tensions involved in the process, the gratitude for her sister's organizational efforts, and speculates on how their ancestors might feel about their efforts to preserve family history. The chapter closes with mutual appreciation between Tess and Tom for sharing and recording these family stories.

story.title: Tess Rossow narrator TBrown 2026-03-01
story.description: The interview is part of the Emmet Orchards Oral History Project, focusing on Tess Rossow's family history related to orchards in Emmet. Tess discusses her family’s multigenerational connection to the orchards, her father’s early demise, and the changes over the years as the family moved from Idaho to other places. Tess reflects on her grandmother's strong values and their influence on her, despite initial resentment. Additionally, Tess shares insights into her childhood, career, personal life, and maintaining strong family ties. The conversation also touches on preserving family histories and traditions through photographs and memorabilia.
